Here is the complete algorithm:
1. Read n,m. Build adjacency list from m edges.
2. Create  `color`  array of size n+1,all−1.
3. For each pupil i from 1 to n: if  `color[i]==-1`,run BFS,coloring with 0 and 1. If conflict found,print "IMPOSSIBLE" and exit.
4. If no conflicts,print  `color[1]+1`,`color[2]+1`,...,`color[n]+1` . That is it. The algorithm you already know produces the output directly. This runs in O(V+E)time and uses O(V)space.

Here is the full solution:
Pseudocode


```
function buildTeams():
    read n,m
    graph :=array of n+1 empty lists
    for i from 1 to m:
        read a,b
        graph[a].append(b)graph[b].append(a)color :=array of size n+1,all 0
        for i from 1 to n:
            if color[i]=0 then
                if not bfs(i,1,graph,color)then
                    print "IMPOSSIBLE"
                    return
    for i from 1 to n:
        print color[i]

```

The BFS function is the same as before: color nodes,check conflicts. On success,output each color plus one.
This runs in O(V+E)time and uses O(V)space.

let's trace n=4,m=3,edges:(1,2),(2,3),(3,4). This is a path: 1-2-3-4. BFS from 1:  `color[1]=0` . Neighbor 2:  `color[2]=1` . Neighbor of 2: 1(already 0,good),3(`color[3]=0`).
Neighbor of 3: 2(already 1,good),4(`color[4]=1`). Final:  `color=[-1,0,1,0,1]` . Output: "1 2 1 2"(adding 1 to each). Team 1: pupils 1,3. Team 2: pupils 2,4.

What did this problem add?
1. Output the partition,not yes/no. The color array is the answer.
2. Competitive programming I/O: read from stdin,print to stdout.
3. Convert colors 0/1 to team labels 1/2 with  `+1` .
4. Handle the impossible case explicitly with "IMPOSSIBLE".
Three problems,same algorithm. The framing changes,but the core bipartite detection stays constant. You now have a template that works across platforms.

Bipartite graphs reveal special algorithms. The most famous is maximum bipartite matching. Given a bipartite graph,find the largest set of edges such that no node appears twice. This solves job assignment,resource allocation,and scheduling problems. You will learn matching algorithms in later sections.
For now,know that detecting bipartite structure is the first step. If the graph is not bipartite,matching algorithms do not apply.

You have learned bipartite graphs:
1. A bipartite graph can be split into two groups with all edges between groups.
2. Equivalently,you can 2-color it with no adjacent same-colored nodes.
3. A graph is bipartite if and only if it has no odd cycles.
4. Detection uses BFS or DFS with alternating colors. Same-color neighbors mean not bipartite.
5. The pattern appears in many problems: "split into two groups where X" often means bipartite.
You solved three problems with the same core algorithm. Now go practice more in the problemset.

A Tree is a graph that satisfies three simple rules:
1. Connected: You can get from any node to any other node.
2. No cycles: There is exactly one path between any two nodes.
3. n nodes means n−1 edges.
These three properties are connected. If you have any two of them,you get the third for free. For instance,if a graph is connected and has n−1 edges,it cannot have cycles. Trees are the simplest connected graphs. They form the foundation of many data structures and algorithms in computer science.

![photo_۲۰۲۵-۱۲-۲۶_۰۱-۲۹-۴۴.jpg](https://repovive.com/api/images/694dcc75ebaffab891e26c18)

In a standard graph,nodes float in space. In a tree,you usually imagine gravity pulling everything down from a root node at the top.
This vertical layout makes parent-child relationships clear. The root is at the top,its children below it,their children below them,and so on. Leaves hang at the bottom.
It helps you think about depth,height,ancestors,and descendants. When you draw a tree this way,the structure becomes intuitive and easy to reason about.

Because trees have no cycles,you can simplify your DFS. You do not need a visited array. Instead,avoid going back to the parent node you came from.
Pass the parent as a parameter in your recursive function. When exploring neighbors,skip the parent. This guarantees you will not revisit nodes or get stuck in loops.
This small trick makes tree traversal cleaner and faster than general graph traversal. No extra memory for tracking visited nodes,only one pointer to the parent.

There are two ways to measure vertical distance in a tree.
1. Depth: Distance from the root to a node.
The root has depth 0. Its children have depth 1. Their children have depth 2. And so on.
2. Height: Distance from a node to the deepest leaf below it.
Leaves have height 0. A node with only leaf children has height 1. The root usually has the maximum height. Depth goes down. Height goes up. They are inverses in a sense.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 104 Maximum Depth of Binary Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
Problem: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree Task: You are given the root of a binary tree. Return the maximum depth(the number of nodes along the longest path from the root to any leaf). This is a classic tree DP problem. You solve it recursively by asking: what is the depth of the left subtree?
What is the depth of the right subtree? Take the maximum and add 1 for the current node. The base case is a null node,which has depth 0. This runs in O(n)time and O(h)space where h is the height.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 104 Maximum Depth of Binary Tree - Strategy
units.text:
What is the max depth of a node? It is 1(for itself)plus the max depth of its tallest child.
For a leaf,the max depth is 1(itself). For an empty tree,the depth is 0.
This is a recursive definition. The base case is when the node is null(depth 0). The recursive case combines the depths of left and right children.
This pattern applies to many tree problems. Define the answer for a node in terms of the answers for its children.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 104 Maximum Depth of Binary Tree - Implementation
units.text:
Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function maxDepth(root):
    if root is null:
        return 0

    left=maxDepth(root.left)right=maxDepth(root.right)
return 1+max(left,right)
```

This is the simplest tree DP. You do not need extra arrays or global variables. The recursion stack handles everything. Each call returns an integer,and you combine results as you return.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(h)space.

The Max Depth problem introduced you to Tree DP. The pattern is always the same:
1. Define what you want to compute for each node.
2. Express it in terms of the answers for the children.
3. Write a recursive function that combines child results.
Tree DP is bottom-up: you solve for leaves first,then combine results as you return up the tree. Many tree problems fit this pattern: subtree sizes,diameters,path sums,and more. Once you see the pattern,you can apply it everywhere.

Problem: Subordinates(CSES)Task: You have a company with n employees. Each employee except the boss has one direct manager. For each employee,count how many subordinates they have(including indirect subordinates).
This is a subtree size problem. If you root the tree at the boss,the number of subordinates of a node is the size of its subtree minus 1(itself). Compute subtree sizes with a single DFS traversal: the size of a node equals 1 plus the sum of sizes of all its children.

If I am a manager,the size of my team is: 1(Myself)+The total team size of all my direct reports. For a leaf employee(no direct reports),the team size is 1(themselves). The boss's team includes the entire company.
This is classic tree DP. You compute the subtree size for each node by summing the subtree sizes of its children and adding 1. Then,the number of subordinates is subtree_size - 1. The leaf employees have 0 subordinates.

Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
subtree_size=array of size n

function dfs(node,p):
    subtree_size[node]=1
    for child in adj[node]:
        if child !=p:
            dfs(child,node)subtree_size[node]=subtree_size[node]+subtree_size[child]

```

After running  `dfs(root,-1)`,`subtree_size[i]`  holds the size of the subtree rooted at i. The number of subordinates is  `subtree_size[i] - 1` .
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space for the subtree size array.

The Diameter of a tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes. This path does not have to go through the root. For instance,in a tree shaped like a Y,the diameter might be from one tip of the Y to the other tip,passing through the fork.
The root could be anywhere,but the diameter stays the same. Computing the diameter is a classic tree DP problem. You need to think about paths that turn at each node. Each path has a turning point where it changes direction.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
Given a binary tree,find the diameter: the longest path between any two nodes. The path does not have to pass through the root.
This problem is a direct application of Tree DP. You compute height for each subtree and track the longest path that bends through each node. You will solve this problem fully in the Tree Diameter & Center section,where you will compare the DP approach with a Double BFS approach.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Strategy
units.text:
Every path in a tree has a turning point where it changes from going down one subtree to going down another. For each node,the longest path through it is: left subtree height+right subtree height.
The diameter is the maximum of that sum across all nodes. This fits the Tree DP pattern: compute a value per node,combine child results,update a global answer. The height function does double duty by returning height to the parent while updating the global diameter.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Implementation Details
units.text:
The implementation follows the Tree DP template:
1. Define a recursive function that returns height.
2. At each node,compute left height and right height.
3. Update  `diameter=max(diameter,leftH+rightH)`  as a side effect.
4. Return  `1+max(leftH,rightH)`  to the parent.
This runs in O(n)time and O(h)space. You will write the full code in the Tree Diameter & Center section.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Takeaway
units.text:
Diameter shows you the core Tree DP trick: a function that returns one value(height)while computing another(diameter)as a side effect. This "return X,update Y" pattern appears in many tree problems.
The Subordinates problem(CSES 1674)computed subtree size. Diameter computes subtree height and tracks a global max. Both follow the same template: base case,recurse on children,combine results.

What if the tree is not binary? What if a node has 10 children? You could modify the previous approach,but there is a simpler algorithm that works for any tree: Double BFS. The idea:
1. Start from any node and find the furthest node from it.
2. From that node,find the furthest node again.
The distance in step 2 is the diameter. This works because the furthest node from any starting point is always one endpoint of the diameter.

Find the diameter of a tree with n nodes and n−1 edges. Unlike the binary tree version,nodes can have any number of children. The input is an adjacency list,not pointer-based TreeNode objects.
The DP approach from LC 543 still works here,but Double BFS gives a cleaner solution for general trees. You will solve this problem fully in the Tree Diameter & Center section using Double BFS. That section also compares both approaches side by side so you can see when each one fits better.

units.unit_name: CSES 1131 Tree Diameter - Double BFS Algorithm
units.text:
Double BFS finds the diameter in 2 passes:
1. BFS from any node. Find the farthest node A.
2. BFS from A. Find the farthest node B.
The distance from A to B is the diameter. You do not need to try every starting node. The first BFS always lands on a diameter endpoint,no matter where you start. This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space. The Tree Diameter & Center section proves why this property holds and walks through a full example with code.

units.unit_name: CSES 1131 Tree Diameter - Why Double BFS Works
units.text:
The farthest node from any starting point must be one endpoint of the diameter path. Here is the intuition: if there were a longer path elsewhere,the triangle inequality on trees would mean your BFS would have found a node at least as far.
This property is unique to trees. In general graphs with cycles,the farthest node from a random start is not guaranteed to be a diameter endpoint. Trees have exactly one path between any pair of nodes,which makes this argument work.

units.unit_name: CSES 1131 Tree Diameter - Two Approaches Compared
units.text:
You now know 2 ways to find tree diameter:
1. DP approach(LC 543): Recurse on the tree,compute heights,track the maximum left+right sum. Works naturally on binary trees with pointer-based nodes.
2. Double BFS(CSES 1131): Run BFS twice from endpoints. Works on any tree given as an adjacency list. Simpler when you do not need subtree information.
Both run in O(n)time. The Tree Diameter & Center section covers both in full detail with implementations and walkthroughs.

The Center of a tree is the node(or two nodes)that minimizes the distance to the furthest node. If you root the tree at a center,the height is minimized. There can be one or two centers. If the diameter is even,there is one center(the middle node).
If the diameter is odd,there are two centers(the two middle nodes). Finding centers is useful for balancing trees and solving certain graph problems.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 310 Minimum Height Trees - Problem Statement
units.text:
Problem: Minimum Height Trees Task: Given a tree with n nodes,return all nodes that,when chosen as root,minimize the height of the tree. The answer is the center(or centers)of the tree.
You need to find the middle of the longest path. Use the "peeling the onion" algorithm: repeatedly remove leaves until only 1 or 2 nodes remain. Each round,you strip every current leaf and reduce the tree. The nodes left at the end are the centers. This runs in O(n)time and O(n)space.

You could find the Diameter and pick the middle,but there is another intuitive approach: Topological Peeling. Start from the leaves(nodes with degree 1)and peel them off layer by layer. After each layer is removed,new leaves appear. Keep peeling until only 1 or 2 nodes remain. These are the centers. Why?
Because the centers are the nodes furthest from all leaves. Peeling removes the outer layers,leaving the core.

Problem: Same Tree Task: You are given the roots of two binary trees p and q. Return true if the trees are identical(same structure and same values at each node),false otherwise. This problem introduces simultaneous tree traversal.
You walk through both trees at the same time,comparing values at each step. If you find any mismatch in structure or value,you return false immediately. The base case: if both nodes are null,they match. If one is null and the other is not,they differ.

The algorithm has three main checks:
1. If both nodes are null,return true(empty trees match).
2. If one node is null and the other is not,return false(structure mismatch).
3. If both nodes exist,check if their values are equal.
If not,return false. If yes,recursively check left subtrees and right subtrees. You combine the recursive results with AND. Both left and right must match for the whole tree to match. This is a clean application of divide and conquer on tree structure.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(h)space.

This problem teaches you to walk two data structures in parallel. You do not process one tree and then the other. You process them together,node by node. The pattern appears in many tree comparison problems: checking symmetry,finding common subtrees,or merging trees.
You need to mirror your recursive calls on both inputs. Remember to handle all combinations of null and non-null. Missing an edge case here causes bugs. Check both-null,one-null,and both-exist separately.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 226 Invert Binary Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
Problem: Invert Binary Tree Task: You are given the root of a binary tree. Invert the tree by swapping the left and right children of every node. Return the root of the inverted tree. This problem is famous.
It tests if you can modify a tree recursively. The idea is simple: flip the children at each node,then recurse on the children to flip their children,and so on. The base case is a null node,where you do nothing. This runs in O(n)time and O(h)space for the recursion stack.

To invert a tree,you swap the left and right children of the root. Then you invert the left subtree and the right subtree. The recursion does the rest. The base case is a null node. If the node is null,there is nothing to invert,so you return null.
If the node exists,swap its children,recurse on both,and return the node. This is tree modification in action. You are not only reading values. You are changing the structure of the tree itself.

The algorithm is three steps:
1. If the node is null,return null(base case).
2. Swap the left and right children of the current node.
3. Recursively invert the left subtree and the right subtree.
You can swap before or after the recursive calls. The order does not matter because you are processing the whole tree. The recursion ensures every node gets its children swapped. This is a post-order traversal in disguise. You process children first(by recursing),then handle the current node(by swapping).
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(h)space.

Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function invertTree(root):
    if root is null:
        return null

    temp=root.left
    root.left=root.right
    root.right=temp

    invertTree(root.left)invertTree(root.right)
return root

```

You swap the pointers with a temp variable. Then you recurse on the swapped children. The recursion propagates the inversion down the tree. Finally,you return the root.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(h)space.

This problem shows you how to modify a tree in place. You do not create a new tree. You change the existing one by swapping pointers. The pattern applies to other tree changes: flattening a tree to a linked list,rotating nodes,or pruning branches. You need to identify the local change(swap,delete,rotate)and trust recursion to apply it everywhere.
Always return the root after modification. Even if you change the tree in place,returning the root makes the function composable and easier to test.

You have gone deep into trees. - Structure: Root,Parent,Child,Leaf. - Traversal: DFS with parent check(no visited array). - Tree DP: Calculating sizes/heights bottom-up. - Diameter: The longest path(Double BFS). - Centers: Peeling the tree layer by layer.
Trees are effective,but they are static. In the next section,you look at how to manage groups that change over time using Disjoint Set Union(DSU).

In this section,I'll teach you how to measure trees. Not with a tape measure,but by finding the longest path between any two nodes(the diameter),and the most central node(the center). You'll learn two different methods to find diameter: the simple two-BFS approach and the effective DP method.
Both have their uses. By the end,you will solve problems asking for maximum distances,sum of all distances,and tree centers. If you have done the earlier sections on DFS and BFS,this will feel like putting those skills to work.

![1769906614383-7ueorf3v0do.jpg](https://repovive.com/api/images/697ea1b699c1ad48c86c0b15)

The diameter of a tree is the length of the longest path between any two nodes. Not the longest path from the root(that is height),but the longest path anywhere in the tree.
Example: If your tree has nodes A-B-C-D-E in a line,the diameter is 4(the path from A to E). If you add a branch,the diameter might change.
A long skinny tree has high diameter. A bushy tree might have lower diameter despite having more nodes.

If your tree represents a network,diameter tells you the worst-case communication distance. In a company hierarchy,it is how many hops a message might travel. In a file system,it is the deepest nesting.
Diameter also helps you find the center of a tree,which is the node that minimizes maximum distance to all others. That is your best meeting point or server location.
Without knowing diameter,you cannot answer questions like "what is the farthest two nodes can be?" or "where should I place the root to minimize depth?"

Here is the core idea: start BFS from any random node. Find the farthest node from it. Call that node u. Now run BFS from u and find the farthest node from u. Call that v. The distance from u to v is the diameter.
Two BFS runs instead of n runs. That is O(n)total time.
This works because one endpoint of the longest path will always be the farthest node from your starting point. I'll prove why in the next unit.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

Say the true diameter is the path from x to y. When you BFS from a random node s,you will reach some farthest node u.
If u is not on the diameter path,then there is a longer path from s through the diameter to one of its endpoints. Contradiction: u would not be farthest.
So u must be on or reachable-to an endpoint of the diameter. When you BFS from u,you will reach the other endpoint,giving you the full diameter.
This is not a full formal proof,but it is the core intuition.

Here is the two-BFS implementation:
Pseudocode


```
function bfs(start,adj,n):
    dist=array of size n+1,all -1
    dist[start]=0
    q=queue with start
    farthest=start

    while q is not empty:
        u=q.pop()for v in adj[u]:
            if dist[v]==-1:
                dist[v]=dist[u]+1
                q.push(v)
if dist[v] > dist[farthest]:
                    farthest=v

    return(farthest,dist[farthest])function treeDiameter(adj,n):(a,_)=bfs(1,adj,n)(b,diameter)=bfs(a,adj,n)
return diameter

```

Both BFS runs look identical. You are running the same code twice with different starting points.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

You are given a tree with n nodes. Find the diameter: the maximum distance between any two nodes. Input: First line has n. Next n−1 lines each have two integers a and b representing an edge. Output: Single integer,the diameter. Constraints: 1≤n≤2×105 This is the classic tree diameter problem.
Solve it efficiently using two-BFS or DP. Both approaches run in O(n)time and teach you core techniques for tree problems.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

The core idea: start BFS from any node and find the farthest node u. Then run BFS from u to find the farthest node v. The distance from u to v is the diameter.
Why does this work? The farthest node from any starting point must be a diameter endpoint. This is a property of trees that fails for general graphs. The proof involves showing that any path not ending at u or v cannot be longer than the u-v path. You do not need to understand the proof to use the technique.

1. Build adjacency list from the n−1 edges.
2. Run BFS from node 1,track distances,find node u with maximum distance.
3. Run BFS from u,track distances,find node v with maximum distance.
4. Return  `dist[v]`  as the diameter. Two BFS traversals,each O(n). Total time: O(n). Space: O(n)for adjacency list and distance array. This beats the naive O(n2)approach decisively.

Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
function bfs(start,adj,n):
    dist=array of size n+1,all -1
    dist[start]=0
    queue=[start]
    farthest=start
    maxDist=0

    for node in queue:
        for neighbor in adj[node]:
            if dist[neighbor]==-1:
                dist[neighbor]=dist[node]+1
                queue.append(neighbor)
if dist[neighbor] > maxDist:
                    maxDist=dist[neighbor]
                    farthest=neighbor

    return(farthest,maxDist)function treeDiameter(adj,n):(u,_)=bfs(1,adj,n)(v,diameter)=bfs(u,adj,n)
return diameter

```

This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

Example tree: edges(1,2),(1,3),(3,4),(3,5).
First BFS from node 1: distances are {1:0,2:1,3:1,4:2,5:2}. Farthest nodes are 4 and 5(distance 2). Pick u=4.
Second BFS from node 4: distances are {4:0,3:1,1:2,5:2,2:3}. Farthest node is 2(distance 3). The diameter is 3: the path 4-3-1-2.
Notice how starting from an arbitrary node(1)found an endpoint(4),and BFS from there found the true diameter.

Two-BFS is fast and simple. You do not need to overthink it: run BFS twice and return the distance.
The first BFS can start from any node. It guarantees finding a diameter endpoint. The second BFS from that endpoint finds the other end and computes the diameter.
This pattern appears in many tree problems. When you need extremal paths in trees,consider whether two traversals from endpoints can solve it. The technique generalizes beyond diameter to finding tree centers and computing all-pairs farthest distances.

There is another way to find diameter: dynamic programming on the tree. For each node,compute the two deepest subtrees below it. The sum of those two depths is the longest path through that node.
Take the maximum across all nodes. That is your diameter.
This is slower to code than two-BFS,but it generalizes better. If you need to compute diameter while doing other DP on the tree,this fits naturally.

For each node u,run DFS to compute the depth of each child's subtree. The depth is 1 plus the maximum depth of any child. Track the two deepest child subtrees.
The diameter through node u is the sum of the two deepest paths going down. Compare this to the global maximum. After processing all nodes,you have the diameter.
This single DFS computes the diameter in O(n)time. It is useful when you also need subtree information or when the problem combines diameter with other DP states.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
diameter=0

function dfs(node,parent,adj):
    max1=0
    max2=0
    for child in adj[node]:
        if child==parent:
            continue
        childHeight=dfs(child,node,adj)+1
        if childHeight > max1:
            max2=max1
            max1=childHeight
        else if childHeight > max2:
            max2=childHeight
    diameter=max(diameter,max1+max2)
return max1

```

This tracks the top two depths and updates the global diameter.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

Two-BFS is simpler and cleaner for the basic diameter problem. If the problem asks for diameter,use two-BFS.
DP shines when the problem needs more information: diameter endpoints,all nodes' farthest distances,or combining diameter with other tree properties. DP naturally tracks these during traversal.
For competitive programming,learn both. Two-BFS is faster to code under time pressure. DP is more flexible for complex problems. Choose based on what the problem requires beyond the diameter value.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are given a binary tree. Return the diameter: the length of the longest path between any two nodes. The path may or may not pass through the root. This is the LeetCode version.
Unlike the CSES problem which uses adjacency lists,here you have TreeNode pointers with left/right children. The DP approach fits naturally. For each node,combine left and right heights to check if a path through this node is the longest.

questions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Core Idea
units.text:
Since you are already traversing the tree recursively,you can compute diameter during the traversal. For each node,find the height of its left subtree and the height of its right subtree.
The longest path through this node is left height+right height. Compare this to your current best. The function returns height(for parent's use)while updating the global diameter as a side effect. One traversal,two purposes.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Algorithm
units.text:
1. Base case: if node is null,return height 0.
2. Recursively compute left height and right height.
3. Diameter through this node=left height+right height. Update global max.
4. Return 1+max(left height,right height)as this node's height.
Time: O(n),visiting each node once. Space: O(h)for recursion stack where h is height. The recursion naturally handles the tree structure.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Implementation
units.text:
Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
diameter=0

function height(node):
    if node is null:
        return 0
    left=height(node.left)right=height(node.right)diameter=max(diameter,left+right)
return 1+max(left,right)function diameterOfBinaryTree(root):
    height(root)
return diameter

```

Clean and simple. The diameter is tracked in a global variable.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(h)space where h is tree height.

questions.title: Diameter of Binary Tree - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: LeetCode 543 Diameter of Binary Tree - Lessons
units.text:
The DP approach fits naturally into tree recursion. You do not need special setup,track an extra variable.
The pattern: compute something for children,combine for current node,update global answer. This template solves many tree problems: diameter,longest path,maximum sum path.
Returning height while updating diameter shows how one traversal can serve multiple purposes. When you see a tree problem asking for a global property,consider whether post-order DFS with a global variable works.

The center of a tree is the node(or nodes)that minimizes the maximum distance to all other nodes. In other words,the node that is as close as possible to everything.
Every tree has either one center or two centers. If diameter is even,there is one center. If diameter is odd,there are two centers(the middle edge).
Finding the center helps you choose the best root for a rooted tree,or the best location for a server.

If you already know the diameter endpoints,the center is the middle node(s)of that path. Run BFS from one endpoint,track parent pointers,and reconstruct the path to the other endpoint.
Walk halfway along the path to find the center. If the diameter has odd length,there are two centers(the middle edge's endpoints). If even length,there is one center node.
The center minimizes the maximum distance to any other node. This property is useful in facility location problems and network design.

The eccentricity of a node is the maximum distance from that node to any other node. The radius of a tree is the minimum eccentricity across all nodes.
The center of a tree has eccentricity equal to the radius. The diameter equals 2×radius when even,or 2×radius−1 when odd.
These terms appear in graph theory and can help verify your diameter calculations. If your center's eccentricity differs from half the diameter by more than one,something is wrong.

units.unit_name: CSES 1132 Tree Distances I - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are given a tree with n nodes. For each node,calculate the maximum distance from that node to any other node. This extends diameter to all nodes. Naive approach: run BFS from every node,O(n2).
Better approach: use the diameter endpoints. For any node v,its farthest node is one of the diameter endpoints. Run BFS from both endpoints,then answer for each node is max of the two distances.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

units.unit_name: CSES 1132 Tree Distances I - Core Idea
units.text:
For each node,the farthest node from it is one of the two diameter endpoints. Why? Because the diameter is the longest path in the tree,so its endpoints are the farthest apart.
So you can run BFS from both diameter endpoints,then for each node take the max of its distance to endpoint 1 and endpoint 2.
Total: three BFS runs(two to find diameter,two to compute distances from endpoints,but you reuse one).

1. Run BFS from node 1 to find one diameter endpoint u.
2. Run BFS from u to find the other endpoint v.
3. Run BFS from v(or reuse from step 2).
4. For each node x: answer=max(dist from u,dist from v).
Three BFS calls total: O(n). This exploits the fact that diameter endpoints are the "extreme" nodes. Any other node's farthest point must be one of them.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

Here is the solution:
Pseudocode


```
read n
adj=array of n+1 empty lists
for i from 1 to n - 1:
    read a,b
    adj[a].append(b)adj[b].append(a)dist1=bfs(1,adj,n)u=node with maximum dist1

distU=bfs(u,adj,n)v=node with maximum distU

distV=bfs(v,adj,n)for i from 1 to n:
    print max(distU[i],distV[i])
```

This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

Tree: edges(1,2),(1,3),(3,4),(3,5). BFS from 1 gives farthest nodes 4 and 5(distance 2). Pick 4.
BFS from 4 gives farthest node 2(distance 3). BFS from 2 gives distances {2:0,1:1,3:2,4:3,5:3}.
From endpoint 4: distances {4:0,3:1,5:2,1:2,2:3}. From endpoint 2: distances {2:0,1:1,3:2,4:3,5:3}.
For node 3: max(dist from 4,dist from 2)=max(1,2)=2. That is node 3's answer.

The farthest node from any node is always one of the diameter endpoints. This is an effective observation that turns an O(n2)problem into O(n). The pattern: find extremal points first,then use them to answer queries. This appears in many geometry and tree problems.
Diameter endpoints act like "anchors" that bound all other distances. When you see "for each node,find X" in a tree problem,ask whether extremal nodes or rerooting can avoid the O(n2)blowup.

units.unit_name: CSES 1133 Tree Distances II - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are given a tree with n nodes. For each node,calculate the sum of distances from that node to all other nodes. This is harder than Tree Distances I because sum is not max.
You cannot use diameter endpoints. This problem requires rerooting DP: compute the answer for root,then update incrementally when you move the root to each child. When you shift the root from parent to child,some nodes get 1 closer and others get 1 farther. Track subtree sizes to compute these shifts in O(1)per edge.

units.unit_name: CSES 1133 Tree Distances II - Core Idea
units.text:
If you root the tree at node 1,you can compute the sum of distances from node 1 to all others using DFS. That is one answer.
Now how do you compute the answer for node 2(a child of 1)? When you move the root from 1 to 2,some nodes get closer and some get farther.
Nodes in the subtree of 2 get 1 closer. Nodes outside the subtree of 2 get 1 farther. You can update the sum in O(1)using this observation.
This is called rerooting DP.

1. Root the tree at node 1
2. First DFS: compute subtree size and sum of distances from node 1
3. Second DFS: reroot the tree by moving from parent to child,updating the sum using the formula Formula: When moving root from u to child v: - Nodes in subtree of v(size sv​)get closer by 1: subtract sv​ - Nodes outside subtree of v(size n−sv​)get farther by 1: add n−sv​ - New sum=old sum - sv​+(n−sv​)=old sum+n−2⋅sv​
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

Tree: edges(1,2),(1,3),(3,4).
First DFS from node 1: size={1:4,2:1,3:2,4:1}. Sum from node 1: distances are {1:0,2:1,3:1,4:2},total=4.
When you reroot from 1 to 3: nodes in 3's subtree(3,4)get 1 closer,nodes outside(1,2)get 1 farther. New sum=4 - 2+2=4.
This increment formula lets you compute all n answers in O(n)instead of O(n2)naive BFS from each node.

Rerooting DP is an effective technique for tree problems where you need to answer queries for every node as root. You compute the answer for one root,then efficiently update as you change roots. The insight is finding the update formula: what changes when you move the root from parent to child?
This pattern appears in many tree problems: sum of distances,subtree queries,tree DP with changing roots.

Tree Diameter: Find the single longest path. Use two-BFS or DP.
Tree Distances I: For each node,find its farthest node. Use diameter endpoints since farthest is always an endpoint.
Tree Distances II: For each node,find sum of distances to all nodes. Use rerooting DP since the sum changes predictably when you move the root.
Each problem teaches a different technique. Together they cover the main approaches to tree distance problems.

You learned how to measure trees. Diameter is the longest path between any two nodes. You can find it with two-BFS(simple)or DP(integrates with other tree computations).
The center of a tree minimizes maximum distance to all nodes. It is the middle of the diameter path. Radius is half the diameter(rounded up).
For distance queries,you used diameter endpoints(Tree Distances I)and rerooting DP(Tree Distances II). Both techniques turn O(n2)problems into O(n).
These patterns show up in network analysis,tree improvement,and competitive programming. You now have the tools to handle them.

Trees are recursive structures. Each node has children,and each child is the root of its own subtree. You will learn how to compute values by aggregating information from subtrees. Count nodes below you. Find maximum matchings. Calculate path sums.
All by processing children first,then combining their answers. By the end,you will solve tree problems that look impossible at first glance. You will know when to use post-order DFS,how to define state,and how to combine child values.

![1769906691715-m245bg7ykes.jpg](https://repovive.com/api/images/697ea20379c1c35713f5ab86)

Think of a tree with root r. It has children c1​,c2​,…,ck​. Each child ci​ is itself the root of a tree.
The subtree rooted at ci​ contains ci​ and all nodes you can reach by going down from ci​. This recursive structure matters. To compute something about r,first compute it for each child,then combine those results.
The tree breaks into smaller trees. You solve small cases,then build up to the full answer.

Subtree DP processes trees from leaves to root. You compute values for leaf nodes first,then use those to compute values for their parents,and so on up to the root. The pattern:  `dp[node]=f(dp[child_1],dp[child_2],...)`  You combine child answers to get the parent answer.
This works because trees have no cycles,each node depends only on descendants.

Use DFS with post-order processing. Visit children before processing the current node. This ensures you have all child results before computing the parent's result.
Pseudocode


```
function dfs(u,parent)for each child v of u
        if v !=parent then
            dfs(v,u)// Now process u using results from children
            compute dp[u] from dp[children]

```

The recursion naturally handles the tree structure. Post-order means you work from leaves up to the root. This bottom-up flow is the essence of subtree DP.

How many nodes are in the subtree rooted at each node?
For leaves: answer is 1(themselves). For internal nodes: answer is  `1+sum ext{size}[ ext{child}]` . Add 1 for yourself,plus the sizes of all child subtrees. This shows the aggregation pattern: compute children first,then combine. The recurrence is simple but appears everywhere in tree DP. If you understand subtree size,you understand the foundation.

Define what  `ext{dp}[ ext{node}]`  means precisely. Is it the count of nodes in the subtree?
The maximum value? A boolean? Clear state definition is critical. If you define  `ext{dp}[v]`  as "number of nodes in subtree rooted at v",then every computation follows from that. Vague definitions lead to confusion when writing transitions. Write down your state in one sentence before coding. This saves hours of debugging.

Common aggregation operations:
1. Sum:  `dp[v]=Σ_c in children(v)dp[c]` 
2. Max:  `dp[v]=max_c in children(v)dp[c]` 
3. Count:  `dp[v]=|\{c : dp[c] > 0\}|` 
Pick the operation that matches your problem. Subtree size uses sum. Maximum path uses max. The aggregation function determines how you combine child answers into the parent answer.

Each node is visited exactly once during DFS. At each node,you loop through its children to aggregate values. Total work: O(n)where n is the number of nodes. Each edge is traversed twice(down and back up),giving O(n)edge traversals.
Tree DP is fast. No repeated subproblems like in grid DP. The tree structure guarantees linear time. This makes tree DP practical even for large trees with millions of nodes.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

An unrooted tree has no designated root. To use subtree DP,pick any node as root(often node 1). Once rooted,edges have direction(parent to child). Subtrees are well-defined. Some problems care about the root choice(path queries).
Others do not(subtree sizes). If the problem is symmetric,any root works. The choice of root changes which nodes are ancestors vs descendants,but not the tree structure itself.

You are given a company hierarchy as a tree. Each node represents an employee. Node 1 is the CEO. Edges represent direct reporting relationships. For each employee,count how many subordinates they have. A subordinate is anyone in their subtree,excluding the employee themselves.
This is a direct application of subtree size computation. If you can count nodes in a subtree,you can solve this. Compute sizes,then subtract one for each node.

The number of subordinates of node v is the size of the subtree rooted at v,minus 1(do not count v itself). Define  `ext{size}[v]`=number of nodes in subtree of v(including v itself). Then subordinates `[v]=ext{size}[v] - 1` .
This converts the problem into a standard subtree aggregation: compute sizes,then subtract one. This is a common pattern. When the problem asks for something slightly different from what you compute,adjust at the end.

For a leaf node:  `ext{size}[v]=1`(no children,yourself). For an internal node:  `ext{size}[v]=1+sum_{c in ext{children}(v)} ext{size}[c]` . You count yourself(1)plus all nodes in child subtrees. This recurrence is the foundation of subtree DP.
Process children first,then add their sizes. The base case handles leaves. The recursive case handles internal nodes. Together they cover all nodes in the tree.

Run DFS from root(node 1). For each node,visit children first,then compute subtree size.
Pseudocode


```
function dfs(u,parent)size[u] :=1
    for each child v of u
        if v !=parent then
            dfs(v,u)size[u] :=size[u]+size[v]

```

After DFS,`subordinates[u]=size[u] - 1`(subtract the node itself). This runs in O(n)time,visiting each node once. The subtree size is a building block for many tree DP problems.
Space complexity is O(n)for the data structures used.

Here is the implementation:
Pseudocode


```
size=array of size n+1

function dfs(v,parent):
    size[v]=1
    for u in adj[v]:
        if u !=parent:
            dfs(u,v)size[v]=size[v]+size[u]

dfs(1,-1)for v from 1 to n:
    print size[v] - 1

```

Time: O(n)(visit each node once). Space: O(n)for the size array and recursion stack.

Tree: 1o2,3; 2o4,5; 3o6. DFS visits: 4(leaf,size 1),5(leaf,size 1),then 2(size 1+1+1=3). Then 6(leaf,size 1),then 3(size 1+1=2). Finally 1(size 1+3+2=6).
Subordinates: node 1 has 5,node 2 has 2,node 3 has 1,nodes 4,5,6 have 0. The post-order ensures children are done before parents. This ordering is what makes tree DP work.

This problem shows the core pattern: define state(subtree size),write recurrence(sum children plus self),implement DFS. Many tree DP problems follow this exact structure. The only difference is what you aggregate(size,max,count,etc.).
If you can count subordinates,you can solve harder tree DP problems. The aggregation changes but the DFS pattern stays the same. This is your foundation. Every tree DP solution builds on this structure.

Some problems need two DP values per node: one if you include it in the solution,one if you exclude it. Example: maximum independent set on trees.  `ext{dp}[v][0]`=max value excluding v.  `ext{dp}[v][1]`=max value including v.
Transitions depend on whether you include or exclude each node. The constraint(cannot pick adjacent nodes)determines how you combine child values. This two-state pattern appears in many improvement problems.

If you include node v,you cannot include its children(constraint in many problems). So:  `dp[v][1]`=value of v+sum of  `dp[child][0]`  for all children. If you exclude node v,each child can be included or excluded independently:  `dp[v][0]`=sum of  `max(dp[child][0],dp[child][1])`  for all children.
This include/exclude pattern appears in House Robber III,maximum independent set,and similar problems. The insight is that including v forces excluding adjacent nodes.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are a robber planning to rob houses arranged in a binary tree. Each house has a value stored at its node. If you rob two directly-linked houses(parent and child),the alarm triggers and you get caught. Find the maximum value you can rob without triggering the alarm.
This is the classic include/exclude pattern on trees. Track both options at each node,then pick the better one. The constraint(no adjacent nodes)determines the recurrence.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - State Definition
units.text:
Define  `dp[v][0]`=maximum value you can rob from subtree rooted at v if you do NOT rob house v. Define  `dp[v][1]`=maximum value you can rob from subtree rooted at v if you DO rob house v.
These two states capture all possibilities. For each node,you either rob it or skip it. The transition depends on which choice you made. This two-state pattern solves the constraint that adjacent houses cannot both be robbed.

If you rob v(state 1): you get  `value[v]`  plus the sum of not robbing any child.  `dp[v][1]=value[v]+dp[left][0]+dp[right][0]` .
If you do not rob v(state 0): pick the best option for each child.  `dp[v][0]=max(dp[left][0],dp[left][1])+max(dp[right][0],dp[right][1])` .

units.unit_name: LeetCode 337 House Robber III - Base Case
units.text:
If v is null(no node exists),both states return 0. You cannot rob a house that does not exist.
Pseudocode


```
if v is null then
    return(0,0)
```

This base case handles leaf nodes' children and empty subtrees. From here,you build up: leaves compute their DP from null children,parents compute from children,and so on up to the root. The final answer is  `max(dp[root][0],dp[root][1])` .

Write a DFS function that returns a pair(extexclude,extinclude)for each node. Pseudocode:
Pseudocode


```
function dfs(v)
if v is null then
        return(0,0)(left0,left1):=dfs(v.left)(right0,right1):=dfs(v.right)rob :=v.val+left0+right0
    skip :=max(left0,left1)+max(right0,right1)return(skip,rob)
```

Return max of both states at root. Time O(n),space O(h)where h is tree height.

Tree: root 3,left 2(left 3),right 3(right 1). DFS leaf 3(left of 2): returns(0,3). DFS node 2: exclude=3,include=2. Returns(3,2). DFS leaf 1(right of 3): returns(0,1). DFS node 3(right): exclude=1,include=3.
Returns(1,3). DFS root: exclude=max(3,2)+max(1,3)=6,include=3+3+1=7. Answer: 7. Taking the root and both grandchildren gives the maximum.

This pattern appears whenever you have constraints like "cannot pick adjacent nodes" or "cannot select parent and child." The core idea: track both possibilities(include and exclude)for each node. Combine child values based on the constraint. You will see this in maximum independent set,vertex cover,tree matching,and many other tree improvement problems.
The structure is always the same: two states,different transitions.

Some problems ask for the best path in a tree. A path is a sequence of nodes connected by edges. Core idea: every path has a highest node(the node closest to the root that is on the path). Consider each node as the potential highest node.
For node v,the best path through v combines the best paths going down into different child subtrees. You pick the two best downward paths and connect them through v.

To maximize a path through node v,pick the two best downward paths from different children. If v has children with max downward paths p1​,p2​,l…,pk​,the best path through v is v+p1​+p2​ where p1​ and p2​ are the two largest.
You cannot use the same child twice. A path visits each node at most once. This constraint forces you to pick two different child subtrees. In a binary tree,you have at most two children,making the choice simple.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are given a binary tree where each node has an integer value(possibly negative). A path is a sequence of nodes connected by edges. Find the maximum path sum. The path must contain at least one node but does not need to pass through the root.
This is a classic path problem on trees. Every path has a highest node; consider each node as a candidate. Track the global maximum across all possible highest nodes.

questions.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - State Definition
units.text:
Define  `ext{dp}[v]`=maximum path sum starting at v and going down into one of its subtrees(not both). Also track a global variable extmaxPath=best path sum found anywhere in the tree. At each node,update extmaxPath by considering the path that goes through that node using both subtrees.
Return  `ext{dp}[v]`  to the parent for its calculation. The returned value and the tracked answer serve different purposes.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Core Idea
units.text:
There are two values to track:
1. Max path going down from v(used for parent's calculation).
2. Max path through v(candidate for global answer).
Path through v=v+extleftMax+extrightMax(if both positive). Path from v=v+max(extleftMax,extrightMax,0). Return this to parent. The parent can only extend one branch,not both. If the parent used both branches,it would create a branching structure,not a path.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Handling Negatives
units.text:
If a child returns a negative value,do not include it in your path. Taking a negative branch decreases your sum,which is never best. Use  `max(ext{dp}[ ext{child}],0)`  when computing values.
This means "take the child path only if it helps(is positive)." For a leaf node,`ext{dp}[v]=v`(the node value itself). If v is negative,the parent might skip it. The parent compares including v vs taking 0(not going down that branch at all).

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Algorithm
units.text:
Run DFS from root. For each node:
1. Compute extleftMax=max(extdfs(extleft),0).
2. Compute extrightMax=max(extdfs(extright),0).
3. Update extmaxPath=max(extmaxPath,v+extleftMax+extrightMax).
4. Return v+max(extleftMax,extrightMax)to parent.
After DFS,return extmaxPath. The global variable accumulates the best answer seen at any node.
This runs in O(n)time and uses O(n)space.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Implementation
units.text:
Pseudocode:
Pseudocode


```
maxPath :=-infinity

function dfs(v)
if v is null then
        return 0
    leftMax :=max(dfs(v.left),0)rightMax :=max(dfs(v.right),0)maxPath :=max(maxPath,v.val+leftMax+rightMax)
return v.val+max(leftMax,rightMax)
```

Call dfs(root),then return maxPath. Time: O(n)(visit each node once). Space: O(h)for recursion stack where h is tree height. On balanced trees,h=logn. On skewed trees,h=n.

questions.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Walkthrough
units.text:
Tree: root−10,left 9,right 20(left 15,right 7). DFS leaf 15: returns 15. DFS leaf 7: returns 7. DFS node 20: leftMax=15,rightMax=7. Updates maxPath=20+15+7=42.
Returns 20+15=35. DFS leaf 9: returns 9. DFS root−10: leftMax=9,rightMax=35. Updates maxPath=max(42,−10+9+35)=max(42,34)=42. Returns−10+35=25. Answer: 42.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 124 Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum - Lessons
units.text:
Many tree path problems need two values: one for the global answer(path through node),one for recursion(path extending from node). The returned value is what the parent uses to extend its own path. The global variable tracks the overall best path found.
This pattern appears in diameter problems,longest paths,maximum path sums,and other tree improvement questions. Keep them separate. Confusing them is the most common mistake in path DP.

So far you have computed values for one root. What if you need the answer for every possible root?
Example: for each node as root,compute the sum of depths of all other nodes. Rerooting is an advanced technique where you compute the answer for one root,then efficiently update to get answers for other roots. You will see this in later sections. It builds on subtree DP by adding a second pass. The first pass computes subtree values. The second pass computes contributions from above.

Mistake 1: forgetting the base case. If you do not handle null nodes or leaves,your recursion breaks. Mistake 2: processing nodes before children. Use post-order(children first),not pre-order(parent first). The parent needs child values.
Mistake 3: confusing returned value with tracked answer. One goes to the parent,the other is your final result. If your tree DP fails,check these three things first. They are the most common errors.

Trees have no cycles.
This means no circular dependencies. You can compute values in a single DFS pass. General graphs need different approaches(topological order for DAGs,iterative DP for cyclic graphs). Tree DP is clean: process children,combine,done. No need to worry about visiting the same node twice. The tree structure guarantees a natural ordering where children are processed before parents.

Use subtree DP when:
1. The input is a tree(acyclic,connected graph).
2. The problem asks for something aggregated(count,max,sum)over nodes or subtrees.
3. The answer for a node depends only on its descendants,not ancestors.
If you see these patterns,the approach is: define state on nodes,write recurrence combining children,implement DFS with post-order traversal. This structure solves most tree DP problems.

In this section,I'll teach you Floyd-Warshall,the algorithm that finds shortest paths between ALL pairs of vertices at once.
Instead of running Dijkstra n times,you run one O(n3)algorithm that handles everything,including negative edges. You will see how dynamic programming creates the solution,how to detect negative cycles,and how to reconstruct paths. By the end,you will solve all-pairs problems with confidence.

![Screenshot 2025-12-25 at 1.02.33 PM.png](https://repovive.com/api/images/694d7c404343d876635d2fd8)

The naive approach to all-pairs shortest paths is to run Dijkstra from every vertex. If you run Dijkstra n times,the total time is O(n2logn+nm). For dense graphs where m≈n2,this becomes O(n3logn).
Floyd-Warshall gives you O(n3)with no logarithmic factor and much simpler code. It also works with negative edges. The code is about 10 lines compared to Dijkstra's priority queue machinery.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Floyd-Warshall's core insight: can I improve the path from i to j by routing through an intermediate vertex k?
If  `dist[i][k]+dist[k][j] < dist[i][j]`,then going through k is better. The algorithm considers vertices one at a time as potential intermediates. After processing vertex k,all paths using vertices 0,1,…,k are optimal. This builds up the solution incrementally,which is the essence of dynamic programming.

Define  `dp[k][i][j]`  as the shortest path from i to j using only vertices {0,1,…,k−1} as intermediates. Base case:  `dp[0][i][j]=`  edge weight from i to j(or infinity if no edge). Transition:  `dp[k+1][i][j]=min(dp[k][i][j],dp[k][i][k]+dp[k][k][j])` . This asks: is it better to skip vertex k or route through it?
The answer determines the best path. This DP formulation is the foundation of Floyd-Warshall.

The 3D DP state  `dp[k][i][j]`  uses O(n3)space. You can reduce this to O(n2). Notice that  `dp[k+1][i][j]`  only depends on  `dp[k]` . You can update  `dist[i][j]`  in place without storing all k layers.
This is why Floyd-Warshall uses a 2D array and updates it during iteration. The space drops from O(n3)to O(n2). This improvement makes the algorithm practical for graphs with thousands of vertices.

The loop order in Floyd-Warshall is critical: k must be the outer loop. Why?
Because paths using vertices {0,…,k} depend on paths using {0,…,k−1}. If you loop i or j first,you break the dependency order. Always write:  `for k,for i,for j` . Any other order gives wrong answers. This is not a performance improvement,it affects correctness. Get the order wrong and you get incorrect shortest paths.

Floyd-Warshall has three nested loops,each running n times. The innermost loop body is O(1). Total time: O(n3). This is slower than single-source Dijkstra(O(nlogn+m)),but faster than running Dijkstra n times on dense graphs.
For sparse graphs with many queries,Floyd-Warshall is often simpler. The simplicity of the code makes it a practical choice when n≤500 or so. Beyond that,consider Johnson's algorithm.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Floyd-Warshall works with negative edge weights. Dijkstra does not. If your graph has negative edges but no negative cycles,Floyd-Warshall computes correct shortest paths. If there is a negative cycle,shortest paths are undefined(you can loop and reduce cost to negative infinity).
Floyd-Warshall can detect this. This makes Floyd-Warshall more general than Dijkstra,at the cost of higher time complexity.

After running Floyd-Warshall,check the diagonal: if  `dist[i][i] < 0`  for any i,there is a negative cycle. Why?
A negative diagonal means you can start at i,follow some cycle,and return to i with negative total weight. If any  `dist[i][i] < 0`,the graph contains a negative cycle and shortest paths are undefined. You can report this condition and stop. This check takes O(n)time after the main algorithm.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the distance matrix.

Floyd-Warshall computes distances but not the actual paths. To reconstruct paths,maintain a 2D array  `next[i][j]` . Initialize  `next[i][j]=j`  if there is a direct edge from i to j. When you improve  `dist[i][j]`  via vertex k,set  `next[i][j]=next[i][k]` .
After the algorithm,follow  `next`  pointers to reconstruct the path from i to j. This adds O(n2)space but allows you to output the actual path,not its length.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Transitive closure asks: can you reach vertex j from vertex i?
You can adapt Floyd-Warshall: instead of minimizing distance,use boolean OR. Set  `reach[i][j]=`  true if there is a path from i to j. Update:  `reach[i][j]=reach[i][j] ||(reach[i][k] && reach[k][j])` . This computes reachability in O(n3)time. It answers questions like "is there any path from i to j?" without caring about distance.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Use Floyd-Warshall when you need shortest paths between all pairs of vertices. If you only need paths from one source,use Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford instead. They are faster for single-source queries. If you need to answer many distance queries and can afford O(n3)preprocessing,Floyd-Warshall is ideal.
It also handles negative edges(but not negative cycles). The algorithm is simple enough to implement in a contest in under 2 minutes.

units.unit_name: CSES 1672 Shortest Routes II - Problem Statement
units.text:
The problem asks: given a weighted graph with n cities,m roads,and q queries,answer each query: what is the shortest distance from city a to city b?
This is exactly the all-pairs shortest path problem. Run Floyd-Warshall once,then answer each query in O(1). If no path exists,output−1. The constraints allow n≤500,which makes O(n3)feasible. This problem tests whether you recognize the all-pairs pattern.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

units.unit_name: CSES 1672 Shortest Routes II - Why Floyd-Warshall Fits
units.text:
Shortest Routes II gives you q queries for shortest paths. If q is large,running Dijkstra for each query is too slow.
Instead,run Floyd-Warshall once in O(n3)to precompute all distances. Then answer each query in O(1)by indexing the dist array. Total time: O(n3+q). This beats O(q⋅nlogn)when q is large. The pattern is: expensive preprocessing,cheap queries. Recognize this pattern and you will know when to use Floyd-Warshall.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

units.unit_name: CSES 1672 Shortest Routes II - Handling Multiple Edges
units.text:
If the graph has multiple edges between the same pair of vertices,take the one with minimum weight. When reading edges,if there is already an edge from i to j with weight w1​,and you read a new edge with weight w2​,set  `dist[i][j]=min(w_1,w_2)` .
This ensures the distance matrix starts with the best direct edges before Floyd-Warshall runs. Forgetting this can give wrong answers if the input has duplicate edges.

Sample input: n=4,m=3,q=5. Edges:(1,2,5),(1,3,9),(2,3,3). Initialize dist:  `dist[1][2]=dist[2][1]=5`,`dist[1][3]=dist[3][1]=9`,`dist[2][3]=dist[3][2]=3`,rest infinity. After Floyd-Warshall:  `dist[1][3]=8`(route 1→2→3).
Query(1,3)returns 8. This shows how Floyd-Warshall discovers indirect paths that are shorter than direct edges. The algorithm found a better path by routing through vertex 2.

This problem teaches you that precomputation wins when you have many queries.
Instead of running Dijkstra q times,run Floyd-Warshall once and answer queries in O(1). This pattern applies to many problems: if you can afford O(n3)preprocessing,you can answer all-pairs queries immediately. The core insight is recognizing when the query count justifies expensive preprocessing. If q>n,Floyd-Warshall is usually the right choice.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1334 Find the City - Problem Statement
units.text:
The problem asks: given n cities,edges with weights,and a distance threshold,find the city that can reach the fewest other cities within the threshold. Run Floyd-Warshall to get all-pairs distances. For each city i,count how many cities j have  `dist[i][j] <=`  threshold.
Return the city with the smallest count. If tied,return the city with the larger index. This problem adds a post-processing step after Floyd-Warshall.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1334 Find the City - Breaking Down the Problem
units.text:
Find the City has two steps: Step 1: Compute all-pairs shortest paths using Floyd-Warshall. Step 2: For each city i,count how many cities j satisfy  `dist[i][j] <=`  threshold(excluding i itself). Return the city with the minimum count.
Ties go to the larger city number. The Floyd-Warshall step is standard. The counting step is a simple loop over the distance matrix. This two-step pattern is common in graph problems.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1334 Find the City - Counting Reachable Cities
units.text:
After running Floyd-Warshall,count reachable cities for each node:
Pseudocode


```
for i from 1 to n
    count :=0
    for j from 1 to n
        if dist[i][j] <=threshold then
            count :=count+1
            reachable[i] :=count

```

Track the city with smallest count. If counts are equal,pick the largest index(or as specified by the problem).
This appears in LeetCode's "Find the City With the Smallest Number of Neighbors at a Threshold Distance."

Initialize dist with edge weights,run Floyd-Warshall. For each city i,count reachable cities within threshold:
Pseudocode


```
min_count :=n
result :=0
for i from 0 to n - 1
    count :=0
    for j from 0 to n - 1
        if i !=j and dist[i][j] <=threshold then
            count :=count+1
    if count <=min_count then
        min_count :=count
        result :=i
return result

```

Return  `result` . The  `<=`  in the final comparison ensures ties go to the larger city.
This runs in O(n3)time and uses O(n2)space.

Sample: n=4,edges=[(0,1,3),(1,2,1),(1,3,4),(2,3,1)],threshold=4. After Floyd-Warshall:  `dist[0][1]=3`,`dist[0][2]=4`,`dist[0][3]=5`,etc. City 0 reaches cities 1 and 2(count=2). City 3 reaches cities 1 and 2(count=2).
Tied at 2. Return city 3(larger index). This example shows how ties are resolved by returning the higher-numbered city. The tie-breaking rule is part of the problem statement.

This problem shows how Floyd-Warshall enables post-processing on the distance matrix. Once you have  `dist[i][j]`  for all pairs,you can answer complex queries: count neighbors within threshold,find min/max paths,etc.
The O(n3)precomputation pays off when you need flexible queries on distances. The pattern is: build the matrix once,use it many times. This is common in graph problems with multiple queries.

Mistake 1: Looping i,j,k instead of k,i,j. This breaks the DP dependency and gives wrong answers. Mistake 2: Forgetting  `dist[i][i]=0` . This makes the algorithm think the distance from i to itself is infinity.
Mistake 3: Not checking for negative cycles before using dist values. If  `dist[i][i] < 0`,the entire distance matrix is invalid. These are the most common errors when implementing Floyd-Warshall.

Both Floyd-Warshall and Bellman-Ford handle negative edges. Bellman-Ford finds shortest paths from a single source in O(nm)time. Floyd-Warshall finds shortest paths from all sources in O(n3)time. Use Bellman-Ford if you need paths from one source.
Use Floyd-Warshall if you need all-pairs distances or have many queries. Bellman-Ford is better for sparse graphs with one query. Floyd-Warshall is better for many queries.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Johnson's algorithm solves all-pairs shortest paths in O(n2logn+nm)time using Bellman-Ford once and Dijkstra n times. For sparse graphs(m≪n2),Johnson's is faster than Floyd-Warshall's O(n3).
For dense graphs or when simplicity matters,Floyd-Warshall is preferred. It also has better constants and is easier to implement. In a contest,I reach for Floyd-Warshall first if n≤500.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the data structures used.

Floyd-Warshall is O(n3). This is acceptable when n≤400 or so. If n>500,n3 becomes too large(over 125 million operations). Consider Johnson's algorithm or sparse techniques. Floyd-Warshall shines for small to medium graphs or when you need all-pairs distances without complex code.
The simplicity makes it a good choice in competitive programming when n is small. For production systems,measure carefully.
Space complexity is O(n2)for the distance matrix.

Floyd-Warshall uses a 2D array  `dist[i][j]`  of size n×n. For n=1000,this is 1 million entries(4-8 MB for integers). This fits in cache on modern machines. Initialize the array carefully: set diagonal to 0,edges to weights,rest to infinity(use a large constant like 109).
Proper initialization is critical. If you mess up the initial state,the algorithm computes garbage. Memory layout affects performance on large inputs.

To represent infinity,use a large constant(e.g.,109 or 1018). Do not use the maximum integer value(e.g.,231−1),because adding two infinities can cause overflow. After Floyd-Warshall,if  `dist[i][j]`  is still infinity,there is no path from i to j.
Choose your infinity constant based on the problem constraints. If weights are at most 106 and paths have at most n edges,use infinity=109.

For undirected graphs,the distance matrix is symmetric:  `dist[i][j]=dist[j][i]` . When reading edges,set both  `dist[i][j]`  and  `dist[j][i]`  to the edge weight. Floyd-Warshall works the same way for undirected graphs.
The symmetry is preserved after the algorithm. You can save space by storing only the upper triangle,but in practice the 2D array is simpler. Most implementations use the full matrix for clarity.

Build your Floyd-Warshall skills with these problems:
1. Shortest Routes II(CSES): all-pairs queries,direct Floyd-Warshall application.
2. Find the City(LeetCode 1334): threshold-based counting,post-processing the distance matrix.
3. Course Schedule IV(LeetCode 1462): transitive closure variant using boolean Floyd-Warshall.
Each problem reinforces a different aspect. CSES tests basic usage,LeetCode 1334 tests filtering results,and LeetCode 1462 tests the reachability variant.

You have learned Floyd-Warshall,the O(n3)algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths. You understand the DP formulation,loop order(k,i,j),negative cycle detection,and path reconstruction. You have solved CSES and LeetCode problems.
Floyd-Warshall is your tool for all-pairs queries,small graphs,and negative edges. You can now recognize when to use it and implement it correctly. Next section: more advanced shortest path techniques.

Recall that BFS finds the shortest path by expanding in layers. Layer 1(1 step away),then Layer 2(2 steps away),etc. This assumes that "1 step" always equals "distance 1".
But look at this graph: A→B(Distance 10)A→C(Distance 1)C→B(Distance 1)BFS will see A→B immediately(1 step)and declare the distance is 10.
But the path A→C→B is distance 1+1=2. BFS fails here. You need something smarter.

![1769907042182-207l6kwnncr.jpg](https://repovive.com/api/images/697ea3629041be8704abd10a)

In a Weighted Graph,every edge has a "weight"(or cost). It could represent:
1. Distance in kilometers(Google Maps).
2. Time in minutes(flight connections).
3. Cost in dollars(shipping routes).
4. Latency in milliseconds(network routing).
BFS finds shortest paths when all edges have weight 1. With varying weights,you need Dijkstra. It always picks the closest unvisited node,ensuring you process nodes in order of their shortest distance from the source.

Before the main loop,you need to initialize two things:
1. Create a distance array  `dist[N]`  initialized to infinity(a large number like 1018). This tracks the shortest known distance to each node.
2. Set  `dist[start]=0` . The distance from the start to itself is zero.
3. Create a priority queue(min-heap)and push  `(0,start)` . The pair represents(distance,node).
text


```
dist :=array of INF,size n
dist[start] :=0
pq :=min-heap with(0,start)
```

Now you are ready for the main Dijkstra loop: repeatedly extract the minimum and relax neighbors.

By default,C++`priority_queue`  puts the largest element at top(max-heap). You want the smallest(min-heap).
Trick: store negative distances. Or use  `greater<pair<int,int>>`  as comparator:
text


```
priority_queue<pair<int,int>,vector<pair<int,int>>,greater<pair<int,int>>> pq

```

This creates a min-heap of(distance,node)pairs. The pair with smallest distance comes out first. Alternatively,push(-distance,node)and negate when you pop.

This is the core logic. For a neighbor v with edge weight w:
If taking the path through u is shorter than the current best path to v,update it.
Pseudocode


```
if dist[u]+w < dist[v] then
    dist[v] :=dist[u]+w
    pq.push((dist[v],v))
```

Relaxation improves distance estimates. Eventually,all estimates become exact shortest distances. The greedy order ensures you finalize nodes in shortest-distance order.

Here is the complete Dijkstra algorithm:
Pseudocode


```
function dijkstra(graph,source):
    dist[source]=0
    for all other v: dist[v]=infinity

    pq=min-heap with(0,source)while pq not empty:(d,u)=pq.pop()
if d > dist[u]: continue

        for each neighbor v with weight w:
            if dist[u]+w < dist[v]:
                dist[v]=dist[u]+w
                pq.push((dist[v],v))
return dist

```

The  `if d > dist[u]: continue`  line skips outdated entries. When you push a better distance,the old entry stays in the heap. This check filters it out.
This runs in O((V+E)logV)time and uses O(V)space.

You process every edge once. For every edge,you might push to the priority queue. Pushing takes O(logV). Total time: O(ElogV)with a binary heap. Space: O(V+E)for adjacency list and distance array,plus O(V)for the priority queue.
With Fibonacci heap,you can get O(VlogV+E),but binary heap is simpler and fast enough for competitive programming.

Like in BFS,knowing the distance is not enough. You need the path. Use a  `parent`  array. When you relax edge(u,v): if you update  `dist[v]`,also set  `parent[v]=u` .
After Dijkstra finishes,reconstruct by walking backward:  `n → parent[n] → parent[parent[n]] → ... → 1` . Reverse the list to get the path in order. If  `dist[target]`  is still infinity,no path exists. Initialize all parent values to−1 so you can detect unreachable nodes during reconstruction.

In some problems,edge weights are up to 106 and paths can have 105 edges. Maximum distance=105×106=1011. This exceeds 32-bit integer range(≈2×109). Use  `long long`  in C++or 64-bit integers in other languages.
Also set dist initially to a value larger than any possible path,like 1018. Using 109 as "infinity" might not be enough. If you add two distances during relaxation,overflow can silently produce wrong answers.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 743 Network Delay Time - Problem Statement
units.text:
Problem: Network Delay Time Task: You have a network of n nodes with weighted directed edges. Signal starts from node k. Find the minimum time for all nodes to receive the signal.
If impossible,return−1. Run Dijkstra from node k. The answer is the maximum distance among all reachable nodes. If any node is unreachable,return−1. This is a direct application of single-source shortest paths. The "delay time" is the longest shortest path from the source.

The signal travels to all nodes simultaneously. The time it takes for node x to get the signal is the shortest path from k to x. Run Dijkstra from source k. Find  `dist[x]`  for all nodes.
The answer is  `max(dist[x])`  over all x. If any  `dist[x]=Infinity`,return−1. This problem is direct Dijkstra application. No tricks,implement it correctly. Good for practice. Watch out for 1-indexed vs 0-indexed node numbering in the input.

units.unit_name: CSES 1671 Shortest Routes I - Problem Statement
units.text:
You are given a directed weighted graph with n nodes and m edges. Find the shortest distance from node 1 to every other node. If a node is unreachable,output−1.
Run Dijkstra from node 1. Store the distance array. Print the result for each node from 1 to n. Constraints: n≤105,m≤2×105,weights up to 109. Use  `long long`  for distances because the total path weight can exceed 32-bit range. This is the textbook Dijkstra problem with no hidden tricks.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1514 Path with Maximum Probability - Problem Statement
units.text:
Problem: Path with Maximum Probability Task: Edges have probabilities. Find path from start to end that maximizes the product of probabilities. Convert: log(prob)turns products into sums.
Maximizing product=maximizing sum of logs. Or use max-heap Dijkstra variant: relax if  `prob[u] * w > prob[v]` . Start with  `prob[start]=1` . This problem shows that Dijkstra works for any monotone relaxation,not only addition. You just swap the min-heap for a max-heap and reverse the comparison.

questions.title: Path with Maximum Probability - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1514 Path with Maximum Probability - Modifying Dijkstra
units.text:
Dijkstra is usually for "Minimum Sum". Can you use it for "Maximum Product"? Yes! The greedy logic still holds. You want to pick the "best" path so far. Changes:
1. dist array becomes prob array. Initialize to 0(or−1).
2. prob[start]=1.0(100%chance to start).
3. Priority Queue should return the Largest value(Max-Heap).(So use the default priority_queue,no greater needed).
4. Relaxation: if(prob[u] * w > prob[v])prob[v]=prob[u] * w;

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Problem Statement
units.text:
You need the cheapest flight from city  `src`  to city  `dst`  with at most  `k`  stops. Each flight has a price. If you use Dijkstra naively,you get the shortest path by price,but that path might have too many stops. This problem adds a constraint: you can't take more than  `k`  stops.
The state you track must include both the city AND the number of stops remaining. In standard Dijkstra,state is position. Here,state is(position,stops remaining). That's the core twist.

questions.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - State Definition
units.text:
Dijkstra tracks the best cost to reach each position. In a normal graph,position is the entire state. Here,position alone is not enough. Why?
Because arriving at city 5 with 0 stops left is different from arriving at city 5 with 3 stops left. The first state is a dead end. The second can still explore neighbors. Your state is a pair:  `(city,stops_remaining)` . You track the best cost to reach each  `(city,k)`  pair. That's how you handle the constraint.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Algorithm
units.text:
Each heap entry stores three values:  `(cost,city,stops_remaining)` . You pop the cheapest cost first,as always. When you pop state  `(cost,u,k)`,you check neighbors of city  `u` . For each neighbor  `v`  with edge cost  `w`,you push  `(cost+w,v,k - 1)`  onto the heap,but only if  `k > 0` .
If  `k=0`,you've used all your stops. You can't explore further from this state. You might still find the destination from a different path that used fewer stops.
Each state is a(city,stops)pair,so the state space grows to V×k. This runs in O(E⋅k⋅log(V⋅k))time and uses O(V⋅k)space.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Visited Set
units.text:
In standard Dijkstra,once you visit a node,you never visit it again. Here,you might visit the same city multiple times with different  `k`  values. Example: You reach city 5 with  `k=1`  at cost 100. Later,you reach city 5 with  `k=3`  at cost 120. The second visit is worse by cost,but it has more stops remaining.
It might find a cheaper path to the destination. Solution: Track visited as  `(city,stops_remaining)`  pairs. Or skip the visited set entirely and rely on the heap to naturally handle duplicates. The heap ensures you process the cheapest state first,so suboptimal revisits get ignored when you pop them.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Implementation

questions.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Implement Solution

units.unit_name: LeetCode 787 Cheapest Flights Within K Stops - Lessons
units.text:
Dijkstra finds the shortest path in a graph. When you have constraints(like maximum stops),you expand the state space to include the constraint.
Instead of tracking cost per node,you track cost per  `(node,constraint_value)` . The algorithm stays the same. You redefine what a "state" is. This technique appears in many problems: shortest path with limited fuel,shortest path visiting certain nodes,shortest path with color constraints. The pattern is always the same: add the constraint to your state.

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1631 Path with Minimum Effort - Problem Statement
units.text:
You have a 2D grid of heights. You walk from top-left to bottom-right. Each step to an adjacent cell has an effort equal to the absolute difference in heights. The effort of a path is the maximum effort of any single step in that path.
Find the path with the minimum maximum effort. This is not a shortest path by total cost. It's a shortest path by the maximum edge weight along the path. Dijkstra can solve this,but you need to redefine what "cost" means.

questions.title: Path with Minimum Effort - Read Statement

units.unit_name: LeetCode 1631 Path with Minimum Effort - Minimax Path
units.text:
In standard Dijkstra,you minimize the sum of edge weights. Here,you minimize the maximum edge weight encountered on the path. When you move from cell A to cell B,the effort is  `abs(height[A] - height[B])` . The total effort of a path is the largest single effort among all steps.
Example: Path A has steps [1,5,2]. Maximum is 5. Path B has steps [3,3,3]. Maximum is 3. Path B wins,even though both have the same sum.
